**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

*April 2nd, 1887*  
*The Lighthouse, Point Marlowe*

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with a quill that trembles more from the weight of my heart than from the storm that now rages outside. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the sea roars as though it seeks vengeance. I have not spoken to you in five years, and yet, as the night deepens, I find myself compelled to write, as if the very air of this place has turned to ink and parchment.

You were once my greatest joy, my little star, the light in my dark days. I remember the day you were born, how the world seemed to pause and the sea turned to glass. I used to tell you stories of the stars and the constellations, and you would lie in my arms, whispering, “I’ll always come back to you, Papa.”

But time, as it does, has a cruel way of drifting people apart. You left, as you always had a way of doing, with words that cut deeper than any blade. You said I was too stern, too distant, that I never truly saw you. I suppose I was wrong. I thought I was protecting you, but I only made you feel like a shadow.

Now, as I sit here, the storm is worse than any I’ve faced.

I've been a lighthouse keeper for many years, and I've seen many storms. But tonight's storm is different. I've seen many lives lost to the sea, but tonight I fear for my own. I've kept this lighthouse for so long, but I've been alone for five years. I've been waiting for you, my daughter, but you've never come. I know I've been harsh, but I had to be. I've been a lighthouse keeper, and I've been a father. I've been waiting for you, but you've never come. I've been waiting for you, but you've never come. I've been waiting for you, but you've never come. I've been waiting for you, but you've never come. I've been waiting for you, but you've never come. I've been waiting for you, but you've never come. I've been waiting for you, but you've never come. I've been waiting for you, but you've never come. I've been waiting for you, but you've never come. I've been waiting for you, but you've never come. I've been waiting for you, but you've never come. I've been waiting for you, but you've never come. I've been waiting for you, but you've never come. I've been waiting for you, but you've never come. I've been waiting for you, but you've never come

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write to you now with trembling hands, for the sea howls like a beast in torment and the wind screams as if it would tear the very heavens asunder. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see the dawn. Yet, even in this hour of fear, my thoughts turn not to my own fate, but to you—my beloved Eleanor, my daughter, my only light in this dark, storm-tossed world.

Five years have passed since you left, and I have not spoken to you in that time. I have not seen your face, nor heard your voice, nor felt the warmth of your presence. The silence between us has been a chasm deeper than the sea, and I have tried, with all the strength I have, to bridge it. But the tide has been against me, and I fear I have only made it worse.

You were but a child when you left, and I, in my folly, allowed the pride of a man to blind me to the heart of a father. I did not understand the pain you carried, nor the weight of the world you bore upon your young shoulders. I did not know that the sea, which I so loved and revered, could be as cruel and indifferent to us as it is to the waves. I did not know that the light I kept burning for others could be a flame that burned too bright for you.

I have often stood upon the cliffs at

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a banshee at my door, and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very cliffs upon which I stand. I do not know if I shall live to see the dawn, but I must speak, for I have not spoken to you in five years, and the silence between us has grown as vast as the Atlantic.

I have watched the light burn through countless nights, and in each, I have seen the same thing: the world moves on, and people change, and time does not wait for those who linger in the past. I have lingered, Eleanor, and I have grown tired of it. I have not been a good father, and I have not been a good man. I have let the world pass me by, and I have let you pass me by.

You were a child when you left, and I did not understand why. I thought you were angry, that you had been hurt, that you had seen something in the world that I could not see. But I was wrong, and I have not been wrong in many things. You were not angry, Eleanor. You were frightened. You were afraid of what I would become, and what I had become was not the man you knew.

You were right to leave.

But now, as the storm approaches, I find myself thinking of you with a clarity I have not felt in years. I have

My Dearest Eliza,

I trust this letter finds you in good health, though I daresay I shall not be writing to you again, for I fear the night ahead shall be a most cruel one. The sea, as ever, is restless, and the wind howls with a fury that would make even the most seasoned sailor quail. I sit by the window, the light flickering in the dark, and I cannot help but feel the weight of years upon my soul.

Five years, Eliza. Five long, lonely years since you left this house with your mother and your new life in London. I have not heard from you, and I have not written, though I have longed to. The silence between us has grown into a chasm, and I fear it may never be bridged.

You were a fine girl, Eliza. A bright light in the darkness of this lonely keep. I remember the days when you used to come up here with your books and your dreams, and we would talk of the stars and the sea, and of what lay beyond the horizon. You were always so full of spirit, and I was proud of you. But pride, I find, is a fickle thing. It can turn to disappointment when the world does not turn out as one might hope.

I know you have your own life now, your own pursuits, your own family, and I do not wish to intrude upon them. Yet tonight, as the

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**If you are reading this, then I have not survived.**  
But if you are reading these words, it means that, in the cruel irony of fate, I have been spared—*for now*—to pen this letter with trembling hands and a heart heavy with regret and longing.

Five years have passed since you left this house, this life, this *us*. I have not heard from you in all that time. Not a single letter, not a single word. You have not written, nor have I dared to write. We have been separated by silence, by distance, by the cold, unrelenting sea that has always stood between us.

But tonight—tonight is different.

The storm is coming. It is not a mere squall, nor even a tempest. It is a *thing* of the gods. The wind howls like a wounded beast, the sea roars as if it has forgotten its place, and the sky is black as the void. I have seen the lights of the coast, the distant lanterns of other keepers, and I have thought, *perhaps this is it*—that the sea will take me, as it has taken so many before me. That I will be lost to the waves, and my name will be forgotten, as so many have been.

But I am not ready to go.

I have thought about you, Eleanor. I have thought about the last time

My Dearest Eliza,

I write this with a quill that trembles in my hand, not from fear of the storm that approaches, but from the weight of silence that has stretched between us like the tide between the moon and the shore. Five long years have passed since your last letter, and though I have kept the lamp burning night after night, I have kept the heart of a father from its light.

You were young when you left, a girl with fire in your eyes and a spirit that could not be tamed. I told you then that I was not a man of words, that I was a keeper of the light, not of the heart. But I was wrong, and I know it now. The sea has always been my companion, and yet it is the land that has taken from me what I hold most dear.

The storm comes tonight, and the wind howls as though it would tear the very stones from the cliff. I have seen such nights before, and I know that the sea is not always merciful. I have stood on the tower, my hands upon the wheel, and watched the waves rise like the wrath of some forgotten god. I have seen men lost to the dark, and I have seen the light guide them home. But tonight... tonight, I do not know if the light will be enough.

I have thought of you often, Eliza. Of the way you used to sit by the fire and listen to the stories of the

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now with trembling hands and a heart heavy with years of silence. The wind howls outside like a restless beast, and the sea roars with a fury I fear it may not relent. I've spent the past five years in this lonely tower, watching the waves crash against the rocks, wondering if you still remember the sound of my voice or if you’ve forgotten it entirely.

I have not spoken to you in five long years, and I do not know if this letter will ever reach you. But tonight, as the storm gathers strength and the sky darkens with an unnatural gloom, I feel the need to write. It is as though the very air is urging me to tell you what I have kept hidden for so long.

You were a spirited child, full of questions and dreams. I used to tell you stories of the sea and the stars, of how the lighthouse was more than just a structure—it was a promise to the world, a beacon of hope for those lost at sea. You used to ask me why I never left, why I didn’t chase the horizon. I told you it was because I was bound to this place, to this light. But I never told you the truth: I was bound by guilt, by the weight of your mother’s passing, by the silence that grew between us.

I have always believed that words are the last thing to leave when the heart is too full. But

My Dearest Eliza,

It has been five long years since I last saw you, and the nights have grown colder here, not just from the wind that howls across the cliffs, but from the silence that has settled in my heart. I write this with the lantern still burning, its light flickering against the storm that now gathers on the horizon. I do not know if I shall see the dawn.

I have not spoken to you in years, and I have not written, not out of neglect, but out of fear. Fear that the words I might say would not be enough, or worse, that they would not be the right ones. You were always so sharp-tongued and quick to take offense, and I feared that I would say something that would only deepen the rift between us. I have been a poor father, Eliza, and I have not done the things I should have done to mend what was broken.

This evening, as the wind howls and the sea roars, I have felt a strange weight upon my chest. It is not just the storm—it is the knowledge that I may not live to see the morning. The lighthouse has stood for generations, but it is not eternal. I have seen men lost to the sea, and I have seen the light go out. I fear that tonight, the light may go out for good.

I have no regrets in my life, only the regrets of what I did not do. I have spent

**My Dearest Eleanor,**  

I write this with trembling hands and a heart that has long since turned to stone, yet still beats for you. Five years have passed since I last laid eyes upon you, and in that time, the world has turned, the sea has risen, and my soul has been left to drift upon the tides of regret.  

The storm is coming. I can feel it in my bones, a low, growling beast that roars across the horizon like the devil himself. The wind is already howling through the cracks in the lighthouse, and the sea, that old, unfeeling thing, is churning with a fury I have never known. I fear I may not see the dawn. And if I do, I fear I may not see you again.  

You were but a child when you left, Eleanor. A little girl with a mind like the stars and a heart that ached for the world beyond these rocks. I tried to keep you safe, to shield you from the cold winds of this life, but you were always too curious, too bold. You wanted more than the sea, more than the salt and the stone. And so you left.  

I did not stop you. I could not. Not when you were so full of fire and light. But I have missed you, Eleanor. I have missed your laughter, your questions, your stubborn little ways. I have missed the way you would sit by the light

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**April 12th, 1887**

I write this with the flickering light of the lighthouse, the wind howling like a banshee, the sea a beast in frenzy. The storm is upon us, and I fear the night may not end as it began. Yet, I must write—because I have no one else, and because the sea has taken all else.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have not brought the peace I hoped for. You were but a girl when you went, and now you are a woman, hardened by the world, perhaps. I do not know if you still remember the scent of the sea, the sound of the waves, or the way the stars would dance above us when the skies were clear. I do not know if you remember me at all.

I have kept the house as you left it, the same as you left it. The walls are still bare, the hearth still cold. I have not spoken of you, nor have I spoken to anyone. The lighthouse is my only companion, and its light is my only hope.

But tonight is different. The wind is not the same. The sea is not the same. I have seen the sky change, and I fear the storm may be more than mere weather. I have seen the light flicker, and I fear it may not hold. I have heard the waves roar

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**

*April 1st, 1872*

My Dearest Eliza,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you angry. Five years is a long time to go without a word, and I have no excuse—none at all. I’ve been meaning to write, to explain, to apologize, but the sea has always been louder than my voice, and the storms have always been more persistent than my silence.

The wind howls outside like a banshee, and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury that feels almost… human. I’ve seen the sky darken, the clouds twist like writhing serpents, and I know the storm is coming. I’ve been preparing the lantern, the oil, the matches, and the ropes. I’ve even lit a candle to keep the dark at bay. But I’m not sure I’ll survive it, Eliza. I’m not sure I’ve ever been so afraid.

You were twelve when you left, and I didn’t understand then why you ran. You were always so quiet, so distant, like you were waiting for something that never came. I thought I was protecting you, keeping you safe from the world. I thought I was doing the right thing. But now, as I sit here, the lantern’s light flickering like a heartbeat, I wonder if I was only keeping you from *me*.

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper to His Estranged Daughter**

*October 12th, 1893*

My Dearest Eleanor,

I write to you now with a hand that trembles not from cold, but from the weight of years unspoken. The wind howls like a wounded beast outside, and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very stones of this cliff. Tonight, the storm comes with a fury I have not seen since the day I first lit this lamp, and I fear the waves may yet take me from this world before I can say what I must.

You were once my light, Eleanor—bright, clever, and full of a fire that made the whole world seem smaller. But time and distance have dimmed that flame, and I have been too proud, too stubborn, to reach out. I told myself I would not be the one to ask for forgiveness. But now, as the storm gathers strength, I find I cannot bear the thought of being forgotten by you, even in this final hour.

You were not the only one who left. I, too, chose to walk away. I told myself it was for your own good, that you would grow and find your own path. But I was wrong. I have spent these five years watching the world change, while I remained here, a stone in the sea, unable to move.

I have no regrets about the life I have led, but I do regret the life

I've been a lighthouse keeper for thirty years, and I've seen many storms. But this one... this one feels different. I know I won't make it through the night. I've been meaning to write this letter for years, but I've never found the right words. I hope you're well, my dear. I hope you've found happiness. I hope you've found peace. I hope you've found love. I hope you've found a better life than the one I could give you. I hope you've found a family. I hope you've found forgiveness. I hope you've found a way to let go. I hope you've found a way to forget. I hope you've found a way to move on. I hope you've found a way to live. I hope you've found a way to forgive me. I hope you've found a way to forget me. I hope you've found a way to live without me. I hope you've found a way to live on. I hope you've found a way to let go of the past. I hope you've found a way to let go of me. I hope you've found a way to let go of the pain. I hope you've found a way to let go of the shame. I hope you've found a way to let go of the guilt. I hope you've found a way to let go of the sorrow. I hope you've found a way to let go of

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I write to you now with a trembling hand and a heart heavy with longing, for the winds howl like the very soul of the sea, and the sky weeps with thunder. The storm is upon us, and I fear I may not see the dawn. Yet, as I stand here, alone in this lighthouse, I find myself compelled to speak to you—though it may be the last time.

Five years have passed since you left, Eliza. Five long years of silence, of unanswered letters, of a heart that once beat with love for you. I have tried to forget, to let time mend what was broken, but the memory of your face, your voice, your laughter, lingers still like the ghost of a lighthouse beam in the dark. I have kept the lamp burning for you, even when the world outside seemed to fade.

The sea is fickle, and so am I. I have spent these years tending to the light, watching the waves crash against the rocks, whispering to the wind as it howled through the cliffs. It has been my solace, my companion, my burden. But in its silence, I have heard the echoes of your absence. I have been so afraid to reach out, to speak, to say the words that would bring you back. And yet, now, as the storm gathers strength, I find that I cannot bear the silence any longer.

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a wounded beast and the sea roars as if it would swallow the very earth. The lamps in the tower flicker with a feeble light, and I fear that even the light might fail us before the dawn.

Five years have passed since you last set foot in this house, and five years have passed since I last heard your voice. I have not spoken to you, nor have you spoken to me. The silence between us has grown as thick as the fog that clings to these rocks at dawn.

I have always been a man of duty, Eleanor. I have tended this lighthouse for over thirty years, and I have done so with unwavering resolve. But I have never been able to tend to the heart that once beat in this very home.

You were a child of light, and I, a man of shadow. You sought the world beyond these cliffs, while I remained rooted in the duty I had sworn. I do not know if you remember the day you left, or if you remember the day you chose to walk away from me. I do not know if you still remember the way your mother's voice used to echo through these halls, or if you remember the way I used to sing to you as you slept.

But tonight, as the storm gathers strength, I find myself thinking of you more than I have in

My Dearest Eliza,

I write to you now, in the dim glow of the lighthouse’s single, flickering lamp, as the wind howls like a wounded beast across the moor and the sea roars as though it would swallow the very heavens. The storm is upon us, and I am no longer certain that the rocks and the waves will be the only things to claim me tonight. Yet, in this hour of uncertainty, I find myself compelled to write, not to the sea or the wind, but to you—my daughter, my Eliza.

Five years have passed since you left this house, and five years have been a long time to wait for a letter, or for a word, or even for a glance from your dear old father. I have not spoken to you in that time, and I fear I have not been worthy of your forgiveness. I know not if you still remember me, or if your heart has turned from the man who once held you close in his arms and who once vowed to protect you from all the world.

You were but a child when you left, and I was a fool to let you go. I had hoped that the sea would carry you to a better place, a brighter shore, where you might grow into the woman you were always meant to be. But I fear I was mistaken. The sea does not always bring fortune, and sometimes it brings only the cold, the dark, and the silence of a world that

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

If you are reading this, it means that the sea has taken me, and perhaps it has taken more than I can say. I have spent the last five years in this lonely tower, watching the waves and the wind, and I have not spoken to you in all that time. But tonight, the storm is coming—*the like of which has not been seen in decades*—and I fear that I may not see morning.

I have tried, you know, to write to you before. I have tried to send letters through the post, and once, I even sent a small package of dried fish and a bit of wool from my own coat, in the hope that it might bring you a small comfort. But you never answered. You never wrote back. And I did not dare write again, for I feared what might come of it.

I have not forgotten you, Eleanor. I have not forgotten the day you left. I have not forgotten the way your eyes turned away when you heard that I had been appointed to this post, and not to the one in Scarborough, where you had hoped I would be. You said you would come to visit, but you never did. And I—*I was too proud to write.*

But now, as the wind howls through the cracks in the stone and the sea roars like some beast awakened from a long slumber, I feel the weight of the years settle upon me. I have

My Dearest Eliza,

I hope this letter finds you well, though I fear it may find you in a far different state than I, for I write with a heart heavy as the sea in a storm. The wind howls like a beast unleashed, and the waves claw at the cliffs with a fury that speaks of divine wrath. I have lit the lamp, as I have done for thirty years, and though the light shines, I wonder if it will be seen. I wonder if I will be seen.

Five years have passed since we last spoke, and I have not written, nor have you answered. I know you would not have wished to hear from me. You were young when I cast you out, and I was a man hardened by years of solitude and the weight of duty. I did not mean to wound you, but I did. And for that, I ask for your forgiveness, not as a father, but as a man who has come to understand the cost of silence.

The storm comes tonight, and I have no doubt it will be the end of me. I have seen such storms before, and I know their mercy is a cruel one. The sea does not care for the lives it takes. It does not weep for the souls it swallows. I have stood on these rocks, my hands blistered from the salt, my bones aching with the cold, and I have whispered prayers to a God I no longer believe in. But tonight,

**My Dearest Eliza,**

I hope this letter finds you in better health and happier spirits than I have been of late. It has been five long, weary years since we last spoke, and I find myself writing with a heart heavy with both sorrow and longing. The sea has always been my constant companion, but tonight, it feels as though it has turned against me. A storm is coming—one of the fiercest I’ve seen in many a year. The wind howls like a wounded beast, and the waves claw at the cliffs with a fury that makes my bones tremble. I fear I may not see dawn.

I have not written to you in so long, and I know I have no right to reach out again. I was wrong to leave you, to abandon you to your own fate when I should have stayed. I was a fool, Eliza, and I have carried the weight of that foolishness on my shoulders every day since. I thought I was protecting you, that I was doing what was best for you, but I was wrong. I should have been there, standing beside you, not leaving you to face the world alone.

I have not forgotten the days we spent together, the nights we shared by the fire, the way you would sit by the window and watch the stars as if they were a part of you. You were my light, my little star, and I let it fade. I let you go, and I have not been

My Dearest Eliza,

It has been five long years since I last set eyes upon you, and yet, the weight of your absence has never been heavier upon my shoulders. I write this letter not with the hope of rekindling what we once were, but with the hope that you may, in some small way, understand the man I have become.

You remember the nights when I would sit by the fire, reading aloud to you, and the way the sea would whisper its secrets through the glass of the lighthouse. I used to think that the sea was a companion, a friend, and that its storms were but a passing tempest. Now I see that it is not so. The sea is a cruel and indifferent mistress, and it does not care for the hearts of men.

Tonight, the wind howls like a beast in pain, and the waves crash against the rocks with a fury I have never known. I have spent my life watching the sea, and I have learned that even the most steadfast lighthouse can be swallowed by the tide. I do not know if I will see dawn.

I have not written to you because I did not know how. I thought that if I waited long enough, you would forget me, or perhaps you would come back. But you have not. And I have not. The days have bled into one another, and the years have passed like the slow drip of water from a cracked cistern.

I have not told

**To My Dearest Eleanor,**

I write this with trembling hands, for the wind howls like a vengeful spirit through the cracks of this old lighthouse, and the sea roars with a voice that makes me think of you. Five long years have passed since you left, and I have not spoken to you, not even in the silence of the night. But tonight, the storm is coming, and I fear I may not see the dawn.

You were but a child when you ran from this place, from me, from the life I tried to give you. I built this lighthouse with my own hands, stone by stone, and I raised you in the light of its beacon. I believed in you, Eleanor. I believed in a better future for you, and I gave you everything. Yet you chose the world beyond, and I chose to keep the light burning, even when it meant loneliness.

I do not know if you will read this, for I have no way to send it to you. But if you do, know this: I have not forgotten you. I have not stopped loving you. The sea may rage, the wind may howl, but I will not let the darkness take me. I will keep the light burning, just as I kept the fire of hope alive within me.

I pray that one day, when the storm has passed and the sea has calmed, you will return. Not to this lighthouse, perhaps, but

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I sit by the window, the wind howling like a banshee, the sea roaring as though it would devour the very stones of this cliff. The storm is upon us, and I fear it may take more than the lighthouse—more than this old, battered building that has stood for thirty years. I have not written to you in five years, and now, with the tempest at our door, I find myself compelled to do so. Perhaps it is the wind, perhaps it is the weight of silence, but I must speak.

You were a fine girl, Eleanor. I remember the day you left, the way your eyes were filled with fire and your voice with fury. You said I had no soul, that I was a man of stone and salt, that I had no heart. I did not answer you. I merely looked out to sea, for that is all I have ever known. You were right, in a way. I have no heart to speak of, only this lighthouse, this cold, unyielding stone, and the sea that has always been my companion.

But I have thought of you, Eleanor. I have thought of you more than I have thought of the sky or the tides. I have watched the stars from this very window, and I have thought of you beneath them, lost in some distant city, far from this place of salt and solitude. I have wondered if you ever thought of me

**April 12th, 1887**  
**St. Agnes Lighthouse, Isle of Wight**

My dearest Eleanor,  

I hope this letter finds you well, though I dares not imagine what you might be doing in that London town of yours. Five years have passed since the last time I laid eyes on you, and yet it feels like a lifetime. I write this by the dim glow of the lighthouse lamp, its steady hum a comfort in these restless nights. But tonight—*tonight*—the sea is restless, and the wind howls like a wounded beast. I fear the storm will take more than the rocks.

You were twelve when you left, a little girl with a face like moonlight and a temper like thunder. I remember the day you ran from the house, your small fists clenched, your eyes wide with some terrible anger. "You don’t love me," you’d screamed, and I couldn’t answer. I didn’t know how to tell you that love is not always gentle, and that sometimes it takes a lifetime to understand.  

I’ve kept the lighthouse for over twenty years now, since the old keeper died in that terrible fire. I’ve seen the sea rage and the sky split open, but never have I felt so alone as I do now. The storm is coming, and I’m not sure I’ll survive it. I’ve been hearing the wind whisper your name in the night

**To My Dearest Clara,**

**April 12th, 1897**

**The Lighthouse, Skerryvore Head**

**My Dearest Clara,**

I hope this letter finds you well, though I daresay it will not be the same as the ones we once exchanged. These days, I find myself writing more to the sea than to you. The waves are a better listener, I think. They never judge, nor do they leave me hanging.

It has been five long years since we last spoke, and I must confess, I have not been the same since. The loneliness of this place, the endless hours of solitude, have worn on me more than I care to admit. I’ve taken to speaking to the lighthouse itself, as if it might understand. “You should have seen her, old fellow,” I tell the stone walls, “she was a light in the darkest of nights.”

But I digress. You are the one I should be writing to, not the rock. I suppose I must begin by apologizing, though I do not know if words can ever make up for the silence we have shared. I have spent so many years trying to outrun the memories of your last visit, the way your eyes filled with tears as you stood at the edge of the cliff, the way you whispered, “I love you, Father,” and then turned away without looking back.

I have not been a good father, Clara

I've been a lighthouse keeper for thirty years, and I've never seen a storm like this. The waves are crashing against the rocks like a thousand thunderclaps, and the wind howls like a banshee. I'm not sure I'll make it through the night, but I have to write this letter before it's too late.

I've tried to reach out to you before, but the sea has always been in the way. I know you think I'm a cruel man, but I've only ever wanted the best for you. You were just a child when you left, and I was too proud to ask you to stay.

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

**Letter from the Lighthouse Keeper**

*The wind howls like a wounded beast, rattling the windows of this old stone tower. I've been here for twenty years, and never once have I thought to write a letter—until tonight. The sea roars, and I feel the weight of the world pressing down on me. I need to get this out before the storm swallows everything, including me.*

**My Dearest Eleanor,**

I haven’t written your name in five years, not since the day you left this house with your mother’s suitcase and that damned letter tucked inside. I still remember the way you looked—your eyes, so full of fire and fury, and the way you turned your back to me as if I were some cursed thing. You said you couldn’t stay here, that the lighthouse was a prison, that I’d never let you go.

I suppose you were right. I did. I did let you go, and I’ve been trying to forget you ever since.

But tonight… tonight is different. The storm is coming, and I can feel it in my bones.
